title: The Job Interview
summary: Sarah interviews for a position at a small marketing company. Interviewer Mark asks about her experience, strengths, and motivations, and she responds with confidence and thoughtfulness.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Good morning, Sarah. Please take a seat. Thank you for coming in today.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Good morning, Mark. Thank you for the opportunity. I've been looking forward to this.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Let's start with some background. Could you tell me a little about your previous experience in marketing?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Of course. I spent two years working as a content writer for a mid-sized agency, where I was mainly responsible for managing social media accounts and writing copy for digital campaigns. After that, I moved into a coordinator role, which gave me a broader view of how campaigns are planned and executed from start to finish.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: That sounds like a solid foundation. What would you say is your greatest strength in a professional context?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: I think my greatest strength is my ability to adapt quickly. In agency work, priorities can shift overnight, and I've learned to stay calm under pressure and reorganise my workload without losing quality. I also tend to be quite detail-oriented, which I find is essential when you're handling multiple clients at once.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: And what about areas where you feel you still have room to grow?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Honestly, I'd say data analytics. I have a basic understanding of performance metrics, but I know that more advanced data interpretation is something I need to develop further. That's actually one of the reasons I was drawn to this role — I noticed that your team works closely with analytics tools, and I'd welcome the chance to build that skill properly.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: That's a thoughtful answer. Let me ask you about teamwork. Can you describe a situation where you had to work through a disagreement with a colleague?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Sure. There was a campaign where my colleague and I had very different ideas about the direction of the visuals. I thought a minimalist approach would resonate better with the target audience, but she felt strongly that bold, colourful imagery was the way to go. We agreed to present both options to the client and let them decide. In the end, the client chose a version that blended both ideas — and the campaign performed really well. I think that experience taught me that disagreements, handled respectfully, can actually lead to better outcomes.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: I like that. Now, why specifically are you interested in working with us rather than a larger agency?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Smaller teams appeal to me because you can see the direct impact of your work much more clearly. At a large agency, it can sometimes feel like you're just one small part of a machine. Here, I'd have the chance to contribute to something from the ground up, build real relationships with clients, and take on more responsibility earlier in my career. That kind of environment is where I feel I do my best work.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: That makes a lot of sense. We do value initiative here. Is there anything you'd like to ask about the role or the team?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Yes, actually. I'd love to understand how the team handles creative differences internally — is there a set process, or does it tend to vary from project to project?

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Great question. We usually hold a short creative review at the midpoint of every project where everyone can raise concerns or suggest changes. It keeps things flexible but structured. I think you'd find it works quite well in practice.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: That sounds like exactly the kind of structured flexibility I enjoy working in. I appreciate you taking the time to explain that.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Thank you, Sarah. We'll be in touch by the end of the week with a decision.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Thank you so much, Mark. I really enjoyed our conversation.

annotations.meaningVi: mong chờ, háo hức đón nhận
annotations.exampleEn: I'm looking forward to starting the new project.

annotations.meaningVi: quy mô vừa, không lớn không nhỏ
annotations.exampleEn: She works at a mid-sized company with about 200 employees.

annotations.meaningVi: từ đầu đến cuối, toàn bộ quá trình
annotations.exampleEn: He managed the project from start to finish.
A solid foundation in grammar helps you learn faster.
You need to adapt quickly when working in a new environment.
She performs well even under pressure.
This role requires someone who is detail-oriented.

annotations.meaningVi: dư địa để phát triển, cơ hội cải thiện
annotations.exampleEn: Everyone has room to grow, no matter how experienced they are.
She was drawn to the company's collaborative culture.

annotations.meaningVi: đối tượng khách hàng mục tiêu
annotations.exampleEn: You must understand your target audience before designing a campaign.

annotations.meaningVi: xử lý một cách tôn trọng
annotations.exampleEn: Disagreements should always be handled respectfully.

annotations.meaningVi: từ đầu, từ con số không
annotations.exampleEn: They built the company from the ground up.
She was eager to take on more responsibility in her new role.

annotations.meaningVi: coi trọng tinh thần chủ động
annotations.exampleEn: Good employers value initiative in their staff.
The theory sounds good, but does it work in practice?
We'll be in touch once we've reviewed all applications.

grammarNotes.title: Past Simple vs. Past Continuous — Describing background and interrupted actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong đoạn hội thoại, Sarah dùng quá khứ đơn để nêu các sự kiện đã hoàn thành ('I spent two years', 'I moved into a coordinator role') và quá khứ tiếp diễn để mô tả bối cảnh ('where I was mainly responsible for…'). Quy tắc: quá khứ tiếp diễn = hành động đang diễn ra trong quá khứ; quá khứ đơn = sự kiện hoàn thành hoặc chuỗi sự kiện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Sarah uses the past simple for completed events ('I spent two years', 'I moved into a coordinator role') and the past continuous for ongoing background states ('where I was mainly responsible for…'). Rule: past continuous = ongoing background action; past simple = completed event or sequence.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional Structures — Expressing general truths and hypothetical situations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mark và Sarah dùng điều kiện loại 0 và loại 2 để thể hiện ý kiến chuyên nghiệp. 'If you take the time to fix something, it lasts' là điều kiện loại 0 (sự thật chung). 'I'd have the chance to contribute…' là dạng điều kiện loại 2 — 'I would have' diễn đạt tình huống giả định trong tương lai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses zero and second conditionals to express professional opinions. 'If you take the time to fix something, it lasts' is a zero conditional (general truth). 'I'd have the chance to contribute' uses 'would + infinitive' as a second conditional to describe a desired hypothetical situation.

translationVi:
Mark: Chào buổi sáng, Sarah. Mời cô ngồi. Cảm ơn cô đã đến hôm nay.
Sarah: Chào buổi sáng, Mark. Cảm ơn anh đã tạo cơ hội. Tôi đã mong chờ buổi này.
Mark: Hãy bắt đầu với một chút thông tin nền. Cô có thể kể cho tôi nghe đôi điều về kinh nghiệm marketing trước đây của cô không?
Sarah: Tất nhiên rồi. Tôi đã làm việc hai năm với tư cách là nhà viết nội dung cho một công ty quảng cáo quy mô vừa, chủ yếu chịu trách nhiệm quản lý tài khoản mạng xã hội và viết nội dung cho các chiến dịch kỹ thuật số. Sau đó, tôi chuyển sang vị trí điều phối viên, giúp tôi có cái nhìn rộng hơn về cách các chiến dịch được lên kế hoạch và triển khai từ đầu đến cuối.
Mark: Nghe có vẻ là một nền tảng vững chắc. Cô cho rằng điểm mạnh lớn nhất của mình trong môi trường chuyên nghiệp là gì?
Sarah: Tôi nghĩ điểm mạnh lớn nhất của tôi là khả năng thích nghi nhanh. Trong công việc ở agency, các ưu tiên có thể thay đổi chỉ sau một đêm, và tôi đã học cách giữ bình tĩnh dưới áp lực và sắp xếp lại công việc mà không giảm chất lượng. Tôi cũng khá chú trọng đến chi tiết, điều mà tôi thấy rất cần thiết khi xử lý nhiều khách hàng cùng một lúc.
Mark: Còn những lĩnh vực mà cô cảm thấy mình vẫn còn cần cải thiện thì sao?
Sarah: Thành thật mà nói, tôi nghĩ đó là phân tích dữ liệu. Tôi có hiểu biết cơ bản về các chỉ số hiệu suất, nhưng tôi biết rằng diễn giải dữ liệu ở mức độ nâng cao hơn là thứ tôi cần phát triển thêm. Thực ra đó là một trong những lý do tôi bị thu hút bởi vị trí này — tôi nhận thấy nhóm của anh làm việc chặt chẽ với các công cụ phân tích, và tôi muốn có cơ hội xây dựng kỹ năng đó một cách bài bản.
Mark: Câu trả lời rất chín chắn. Để tôi hỏi về tinh thần đồng đội. Cô có thể mô tả một tình huống mà cô phải giải quyết bất đồng với đồng nghiệp không?
Sarah: Được thôi. Có một chiến dịch mà đồng nghiệp của tôi và tôi có ý kiến rất khác nhau về định hướng hình ảnh. Tôi nghĩ phong cách tối giản sẽ phù hợp hơn với đối tượng khách hàng mục tiêu, nhưng cô ấy kiên quyết rằng hình ảnh đậm màu sắc mới là hướng đi đúng. Chúng tôi đồng ý trình bày cả hai phương án cho khách hàng và để họ quyết định. Cuối cùng, khách hàng chọn một phiên bản kết hợp cả hai ý tưởng — và chiến dịch đã thực sự thành công. Tôi nghĩ trải nghiệm đó dạy tôi rằng những bất đồng, nếu được xử lý một cách tôn trọng, thực sự có thể dẫn đến kết quả tốt hơn.
Mark: Tôi thích điều đó. Bây giờ, tại sao cô đặc biệt muốn làm việc với chúng tôi thay vì một agency lớn hơn?
Sarah: Các nhóm nhỏ hơn thu hút tôi vì bạn có thể thấy rõ hơn tác động trực tiếp từ công việc của mình. Ở một agency lớn, đôi khi bạn có cảm giác chỉ là một phần nhỏ trong một cỗ máy. Ở đây, tôi sẽ có cơ hội đóng góp vào điều gì đó từ đầu, xây dựng mối quan hệ thực sự với khách hàng, và đảm nhận thêm trách nhiệm sớm hơn trong sự nghiệp của mình. Đó là môi trường mà tôi cảm thấy mình làm tốt nhất.
Mark: Điều đó có lý lắm. Chúng tôi rất coi trọng tinh thần chủ động ở đây. Cô có muốn hỏi gì về vị trí hoặc nhóm không?
Sarah: Thực ra có. Tôi muốn hiểu hơn cách nhóm xử lý những bất đồng về sáng tạo nội bộ — liệu có quy trình cụ thể không, hay nó thường thay đổi tùy theo từng dự án?
Mark: Câu hỏi hay đấy. Thường thì chúng tôi tổ chức một buổi đánh giá sáng tạo ngắn ở giữa mỗi dự án, nơi mọi người có thể nêu ý kiến hay đề xuất thay đổi. Điều đó giúp mọi thứ linh hoạt nhưng vẫn có cấu trúc. Tôi nghĩ cô sẽ thấy nó hoạt động khá tốt trong thực tế.
Sarah: Nghe giống đúng loại linh hoạt có cấu trúc mà tôi thích làm việc. Tôi đánh giá cao việc anh dành thời gian giải thích điều đó.
Mark: Cảm ơn cô, Sarah. Chúng tôi sẽ liên hệ lại vào cuối tuần với quyết định.
Sarah: Cảm ơn anh rất nhiều, Mark. Tôi thực sự thích cuộc trò chuyện này.

questions.prompt: What was Sarah's first job role in marketing?
options: Marketing coordinator at a large agency
Content writer at a mid-sized agency
Data analyst at a small company
Social media manager at a startup
questions.explanation: Sarah says she 'spent two years working as a content writer for a mid-sized agency.'
questions.hint: She describes her first role before moving to a coordinator position.

questions.prompt: What does Sarah identify as her greatest professional strength?
options: The ability to adapt quickly and stay calm under pressure
Managing large teams across multiple departments
questions.explanation: Sarah says 'my greatest strength is my ability to adapt quickly' and that she can 'stay calm under pressure.'
questions.hint: She mentions what happens when priorities shift overnight.

questions.prompt: What area does Sarah acknowledge she needs to improve?
questions.explanation: Sarah says 'I'd say data analytics' is where she has room to grow, noting she needs more advanced data interpretation skills.
questions.hint: She connects this weakness to a reason she applied for this specific role.

questions.prompt: How was the creative disagreement with Sarah's colleague resolved?
options: The colleague's bold imagery was chosen
Both options were presented to the client, who chose a combined version
A third designer was brought in to decide
questions.explanation: They 'agreed to present both options to the client,' who 'chose a version that blended both ideas.'
questions.hint: The client played a key role in the resolution.

questions.prompt: What lesson did Sarah take from the disagreement with her colleague?
options: It is best to avoid creative disagreements entirely
Respectfully handled disagreements can lead to better outcomes
Clients always prefer bold and colourful designs
Minimalist design is more effective for most target audiences
questions.explanation: Sarah says the experience 'taught me that disagreements, handled respectfully, can actually lead to better outcomes.'
questions.hint: Think about what positive outcome came from the conflict.

questions.prompt: Why does Sarah prefer working at a smaller company rather than a large agency?
options: She can see the direct impact of her work and take on more responsibility
There is less pressure and fewer deadlines
Smaller teams allow her to specialise in data analytics
questions.explanation: Sarah explains that at a smaller team she can 'see the direct impact of her work' and 'take on more responsibility earlier in my career.'
questions.hint: She contrasts feeling like 'one small part of a machine' at large agencies.

questions.prompt: What is the company's process for handling creative differences on projects?
options: The senior manager makes all final creative decisions
There is no fixed process — it varies entirely by project
They hold a creative review at the midpoint of every project
Disagreements are submitted in writing to the director
questions.explanation: Mark explains: 'We usually hold a short creative review at the midpoint of every project where everyone can raise concerns or suggest changes.'
questions.hint: Mark describes it as both flexible and structured.

questions.prompt: Why was Sarah specifically attracted to this role in terms of her professional development?
options: The company offers formal data analytics training courses
The team works closely with analytics tools, which she wants to learn properly
She has always wanted to work in a creative marketing role
The company has well-known clients she wants to add to her portfolio
questions.explanation: Sarah says she noticed 'your team works closely with analytics tools, and I'd welcome the chance to build that skill properly.'
questions.hint: She connects her area for improvement to why this company appeals to her.

questions.prompt: What does Mark say about how the company views initiative?
options: He says initiative is important but must be approved by management first
He says initiative is valued at the company
He says initiative is less important than technical skills
He says the company prefers employees who follow established processes
questions.explanation: Mark says: 'We do value initiative here.'
questions.hint: He makes this comment just after Sarah explains why she prefers smaller teams.

questions.prompt: When will Sarah hear about the company's decision?
options: By the end of the week
questions.explanation: Mark says: 'We'll be in touch by the end of the week with a decision.'
questions.hint: Mark gives a specific time frame in his final statement.

cloze.template:
Mark: Good morning, Sarah. Please take a seat. Thank you for coming in today.
Sarah: Good morning, Mark. Thank you for the opportunity. I've been {{b1}} this.
Mark: Let's start with some background. Could you tell me a little about your previous experience in marketing?
Sarah: Of course. I spent two years working as a content writer for a mid-sized agency, where I was mainly {{b2}} for managing social media accounts and writing copy for digital campaigns. After that, I moved into a coordinator role, which gave me a broader view of how campaigns are planned and executed from start to finish.
Mark: That sounds like a solid foundation. What would you say is your greatest strength in a professional context?
Sarah: I think my greatest strength is my ability to {{b3}} quickly. In agency work, priorities can shift overnight, and I've learned to stay calm under pressure and reorganise my workload without losing quality. I also tend to be quite {{b4}}, which I find is essential when you're handling multiple clients at once.
Mark: And what about areas where you feel you still have room to grow?
Sarah: Honestly, I'd say data analytics. I have a basic understanding of performance {{b5}}, but I know that more advanced data interpretation is something I need to develop further. That's actually one of the reasons I was drawn to this role — I noticed that your team works closely with analytics tools, and I'd welcome the chance to build that skill properly.
Mark: That's a thoughtful answer. Let me ask you about teamwork. Can you describe a situation where you had to work through a {{b6}} with a colleague?
Sarah: Sure. There was a campaign where my colleague and I had very different ideas about the direction of the visuals. I thought a minimalist approach would {{b7}} better with the target audience, but she felt strongly that bold, colourful imagery was the way to go. We agreed to present both options to the client and let them decide. In the end, the client chose a version that blended both ideas — and the campaign performed really well. I think that experience taught me that disagreements, handled respectfully, can actually lead to better {{b8}}.
Mark: I like that. Now, why specifically are you interested in working with us rather than a larger agency?
Sarah: Smaller teams appeal to me because you can see the direct {{b9}} of your work much more clearly. At a large agency, it can sometimes feel like you're just one small part of a machine. Here, I'd have the chance to contribute to something from the ground up, build real relationships with clients, and take on more responsibility earlier in my career. That kind of environment is where I feel I do my best work.
Mark: That makes a lot of sense. We do value {{b10}} here. Is there anything you'd like to ask about the role or the team?
Sarah: Yes, actually. I'd love to understand how the team handles creative differences internally — is there a set process, or does it tend to vary from project to project?
Mark: Great question. We usually hold a short creative review at the midpoint of every project where everyone can raise concerns or suggest changes. It keeps things flexible but structured. I think you'd find it works quite well in practice.
Sarah: That sounds like exactly the kind of structured flexibility I enjoy working in. I appreciate you taking the time to explain that.
Mark: Thank you, Sarah. We'll be in touch by the end of the week with a decision.
Sarah: Thank you so much, Mark. I really enjoyed our conversation.

blanks.explanation: 'Looking forward to' is a fixed phrasal expression meaning to feel excited or positive about something coming in the future.
'Responsible for' is a standard collocation in professional English to describe someone's duties or areas of accountability.
'Adapt quickly' is the collocation used in the passage; 'adapt' specifically means to adjust to new conditions.
'Detail-oriented' is a common professional adjective describing someone who pays careful attention to small details.
'Performance metrics' is the specific collocation used — 'metrics' are measurable indicators used to assess performance.
Mark asks about working through a 'disagreement' — this is the exact word used in the passage.
'Resonate with' is a B2-level collocation meaning to have a strong positive effect on someone's feelings or understanding.
'Better outcomes' is the phrase used — 'outcomes' refers to the final results or consequences of an action or situation.
'Direct impact' is a common collocation in professional contexts, meaning the clear and immediate effect of your actions.
'Value initiative' — initiative refers to the ability to assess situations and take action without being told what to do.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sarah describes turning a creative disagreement into a collaborative outcome. In professional life, is it always best to compromise and find a middle ground, or are there situations where standing firmly by your own vision produces better results? What factors should influence that choice?
